article_title: Valkey 9.1 Adds Database-Level ACLs, JSON Logging, and a Redesigned I/O Threading Model for the Redis Fork
summary: Submission approvable with 1 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

content_preview.title: Valkey 9.1 Adds Database-Level ACLs, JSON Logging, and a Redesigned I/O Threading Model for the Redis Fork
content_preview.summary: The Linux Foundation's Redis fork ships multi-tenant access control, native JSON logs, and throughput gains, cutting per-key memory by up to 10 percent.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Valkey 9.1, the latest release of the Linux Foundation's open-source fork of the Redis in-memory data store, became available on [May 19, 2026](https://valkey.io/blog/valkey-9-1-delivers-improvements-in-security-performance-and-more/), bringing database-level access controls, native JSON-formatted server logs, and a redesigned I/O threading model. The release drew contributions from [over eighty individual contributors](https://valkey.io/blog/valkey-9-1-delivers-improvements-in-secu...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (658 words, within the 400-1200 range). Clear sectioning (Overview / What We Know / What We Don't Know / Analysis) with appropriate technical depth for a release-coverage article. Every paragraph anchors its specifics to an inline-linked source.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 5 source snapshots read from disk and verified (all status 200, no archive fallback). source-0 (valkey.io blog): confirms May 19 2026 release, 'over eighty contributors', database-level ACLs scoping user permissions to specific databases, Lua extracted to a module reducing security surface area, automatic background TLS cert reloading + INFO expiration dates, JSON logging via the `log-format json` directive, new cumulative main/I-O thread usage metrics, I/O threading redesign 'up to 17%', string GET 'up to 30%', XRANGE/XREVRANGE 'up to 30% faster', hardware clock default GET/SET 'up to 3%', 'up to 20%' memory reduction for strings under 128 bytes, 'up to 10%' sorted-set memory reduction, and new commands HGETDEL/MSETEX/CLUSTERSCAN — all verbatim-supported. source-1 (Linux Foundation): confirms the first Madelyn Olson quote verbatim and her exact title 'Valkey project maintainer and Principal Engineer for AWS in-memory databases', the 'per-key memory usage by up to 10% for common workloads' with no tuning framing, the 'database-level ACL system, providing fine-grained multi-tenant isolation within a single instance' phrasing, and the ecosystem facts (Valkey Search 1.2, Valkey Admin GA visual cluster management tool, Valkey GLIDE 2.4 with C#/PHP). source-2 (PRNewswire): verbatim mirror of the LF release; independently confirms the Olson quote and the 10% memory figure. source-3 (diginomica): confirms the title, '19 May' ship date, the 10% per-key memory reduction, the 'Linux Foundation-governed open source Redis fork'/vendor-neutral framing, the second Olson quote verbatim ('Valkey has been using AI agents to automate tedious, time-intensive tasks now, while the wider industry is working on large-scale agentic models'), and the durability/ACID 'road to 10.0' roadmap that the article paraphrases (outside quote marks) as 'full ACID compliance with fast failover is targeted for version 10.0'. source-4 (github.com/valkey-io/valkey/releases): confirms '9.1.0 Latest ... Upgrade urgency LOW: This is the first stable release of Valkey 9.1.' Every direct quote is verbatim and correctly attributed to the correct speaker.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: No hallucinations detected. Both Olson quotes are exact and correctly speaker-attributed with the full title. All performance/memory percentages, the release date, contributor count, ACL/JSON/threading claims, ecosystem version numbers, and the GitHub upgrade-urgency LOW status trace to cited snapshots. The unsupported 'roughly eight months after 9.0' interval was correctly removed (absent from the body). The Valkey blog's '2.1 million requests/sec at 512-byte payloads' headline benchmark was correctly DROPPED — it appeared only as a Phoronix figure elsewhere (Phoronix 403'd/bot-blocked, single-sourced) and the article deliberately closes by noting the release targets operational concerns 'rather than at headline benchmark figures'. The internal cross-reference to /article/2026-03/02-valkey-turns-two-... resolves to an existing published article.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. No sensationalism, no editorializing, no AI self-reference. The 'What We Don't Know' section appropriately flags open questions (migration timeline, durability roadmap) without overstating.
editor_notes.originality: Genuinely new topic. The only prior Valkey coverage is the March 2026 'Valkey Turns Two' retrospective, which covers the fork's two-year history — a different angle, not a duplicate of this 9.1 release coverage.
concerns: diginomica.com is not currently in config/source_allowlist.txt — the single automated warning. It is an established enterprise-technology outlet; the snapshot fully supports every claim attributed to it, and those claims are corroborated by the primary sources (Valkey blog, Linux Foundation press release). This is an allowlist-coverage gap rather than a credibility problem, recorded in the corrections trail for transparency.

The phrase 'full ACID compliance with fast failover is targeted for version 10.0' is a reasonable paraphrase of diginomica's durability/'road to 10.0' discussion but is tighter than the source's own framing; it sits outside quote marks and is acceptable, noted here for the record.
recommendations: Maintainer may consider adding diginomica.com to config/source_allowlist.txt given it is a legitimate, regularly cited enterprise-tech outlet.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, accurate, neutral release-coverage submission with all five sources personally read and verified, both quotes verbatim, all specifics sourced, the speculative benchmark and unsupported interval correctly dropped, and the internal cross-reference resolving. The only issue is the diginomica.com allowlist gap, which is honestly summarizable in a corrections note. Verdict: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS.
